title: Jam-filled doughnuts
subtitle: Fried yeasted dough balls filled with strawberry or raspberry jam
excerpt: Jam-filled doughnuts are round, deep-fried yeasted dough balls injected with jam after frying and rolled in sugar while warm. The dough is enriched with butter and egg yolks for a tender, pillowy crumb. Frying at the right temperature, not too hot and not too cool, is what gives them their pale band at the equator and fully cooked interior.
sourceNotes: Jam-filled doughnuts are a British adaptation of the German Berliner (also called Pfannkuchen), brought to Britain in the nineteenth century. The doughnut became a British seaside and fairground staple in the twentieth century. The filled-after-frying technique, using a piping bag or skewer, is standard in the British tradition. This recipe is synthesised from the established British doughnut-making tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The dough can be made the night before, proved overnight in the fridge, then shaped and fried the next morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 175 degrees C, not oven-baked

glossaryTerms.definition: A properly fried doughnut has a pale band running around its middle. This ring forms where the dough floats above the oil line during frying. The pale ring is a quality mark, not a flaw. To get it, the oil must be deep enough to allow the doughnut to float freely, and the temperature must be 170 to 175 degrees C. Oil that is too hot will colour the outside before the centre is cooked; oil that is too cool will give a thick, greasy crust with no ring.

content.text: is the hallmark of a well-made doughnut. It forms where the dough floats above the oil surface. Control the oil temperature with a probe thermometer; it is the single most important variable. Hot oil gives a dark crust and raw centre. Cool oil gives a thick, doughy shell.

Deep-frying: The oil must reach a depth that allows the doughnuts to float freely, at least 8 cm. Never leave hot oil unattended. Keep a lid nearby. If the oil begins to smoke, remove the pan from the heat immediately.

items.prepNote: or strawberry jam, warmed briefly to loosen

content.text: Combine the 400 g flour, 7 g yeast, 50 g sugar and 6 g salt in a large bowl. Add the 180 ml warm milk and 3 egg yolks and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Add the 60 g softened butter in pieces, kneading each addition in fully before adding the next. Continue for 5 more minutes until the dough is very smooth and pulls cleanly from the bowl. It should feel soft, slightly tacky and elastic. Cover and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until about doubled in size.

Knock back the dough and divide into 12 equal pieces (about 65 g each). Roll each piece into a smooth ball, pinching the base tightly to seal. Place on two lined baking trays, spacing well apart. Cover loosely with cling film and leave to prove for 45 to 60 minutes until noticeably puffed and soft-feeling when gently pressed.

Heat the oil in a deep, heavy pan to 170 to 175 degrees C. Fry 3 or 4 doughnuts at a time, lowering them in carefully. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes on each side until deep brown. The internal temperature should reach 90 degrees C. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on kitchen paper, then roll in the caster sugar while still warm. Cool for 10 minutes. Fill a piping bag fitted with a plain nozzle with the 200 g jam. Push the nozzle into the side of each doughnut and pipe in a generous amount of jam until you feel resistance. Serve within 2 hours.

items.problem: Doughnuts are raw in the centre despite coloured outside
items.solution: The oil was too hot. The outside browns before the centre reaches temperature. Use a probe thermometer and keep the oil between 170 and 175 degrees C. Fry in small batches so adding cold doughnuts does not drop the temperature too sharply.

The oil was too cool (below 165 degrees C) or the doughnuts were underproved. Underproved dough absorbs oil rather than sealing quickly in hot fat. Make sure the proved doughnuts are noticeably puffy before frying.
The oil was not deep enough for the doughnuts to float. Use a pan with at least 8 cm of oil depth, and do not overcrowd; crowded doughnuts sit rather than float.
